summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt discuss DuckLake, their reimagination of lakehouse formats that replaces file-based metadata (JSON, Avro) with a SQL relational database for all metadata management while keeping data files on object stores. The project emerged from implementing Iceberg and Delta support in DuckDB and realizing the catalog database could handle everything more simply. DuckLake offers unique features like data inlining for small writes, file-level encryption with keys stored in the catalog, and cheap snapshots. The format scales from a single local DuckDB file to thousands of compute nodes, and can serve as a backend for the Iceberg REST catalog. They position it as DuckDB's multiplayer mode, fundamentally flipping the traditional architecture to make client-side compute the primary player.
key_points: DuckLake replaces file-based metadata layers (JSON, Avro files) with a SQL database, reducing query round trips from ~7 (Iceberg) to 2 and cutting massive complexity
Data inlining stores small writes directly in the metadata database, making them immediately visible without the buffering delays typical of streaming into lakehouse formats
Per-file encryption with keys stored in the metadata catalog enables putting data on completely untrusted storage while maintaining security
The concept separates from the DuckDB implementation—DuckLake as a protocol can back Spark, Trino, or the Iceberg REST catalog itself
DuckLake snapshots are just rows in a database rather than expensive file operations, enabling thousands of snapshots without performance degradation
money_quotes: There is this catalog up there that has a database, maybe we should use this for all.

It takes this traditional two-tier architecture or three-tier architecture and flips it around where your client or your local machine is no longer the smallest light in the architecture, but it's suddenly actually the prominent player because it's where the compute happens.

You switch on encryption and we will generate a unique encryption key for every Parquet file we write out to object storage and actually store the key in the metadata catalog. And so what you now have is you can put your DuckLake on a completely untrusted storage and whoever has access to it can do absolutely nothing with those files.

What surprised me the most was that it wasn't that hard. Like when I first got acquainted with Lakehouse formats, I thought this was very complex and I think it is. But then when we got started on Duck Lake, at every step of the way, it actually just kind of worked.
If the ecosystem maybe admittedly or unadmittedly adopt some of these ideas, we also consider this a huge success. For us, we care really deeply about how people manage data.
There's still such a huge gap between what is kind of possible and what is the daily reality of the data practitioners. It's almost like half of the population has to drive a car from the 1920s.
People tell you databases take 10 years and they may be right.
It's not like you need 15 Docker containers, you need DuckDB and that's enough to basically run a local instance.
key_insight: By replacing file-based metadata with a SQL database, DuckLake dramatically simplifies the lakehouse stack while enabling features like instant small writes, cheap snapshots, and file-level encryption that are difficult or impossible with existing formats.
